text: The surgeon continues dissection in the retrosternal space, gradually expanding the view. Occasionally, a portion of the diaphragm is cut to increase the surgical field, while carefully deflecting from the middle to avoid tearing small blood vessels.

The surgeon opens the right pleural cavity and places a 5mm additional port in the fifth intercostal space, providing a clear view of the right phrenic nerve and enhancing operability in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects the right epiphramic fat pads from the diaphragm and pericardium. After verifying the position of the left phrenic nerve, the resection line is determined on the caudal side. The fat and thymic tissue are lifted off the pericardium, exposing the junction of the right and left brachiocephalic veins.

The surgeon pulls the thymus tissue towards the caudal side and dissects the right superior horn and surrounding fat tissue using a light gasser V in the left hand. The forceps and light gasser Maryland through the additional port facilitate dissection at an optimal angle. Mid-veins are sealed using ligature Maryland. The left superior horn is identified and divided safely, emphasizing the importance of both right and left approaches.

The surgeon achieves a clear view of the right brachiocephalic artery, trachea, left common carotid artery, and right brachiocephalic vein. The thymus specimen is then placed in a plastic bag and removed through the sub-xiphoid incision.
